title: Task actor provenance logging in daily contracts
description: Add an `actor` field to task completion state so that when a daily contract task is verified as done, the system records which agent type actually executed it — not just whether it passed the verify function.
rationale: The reading note's core insight: when friction drops, the *actor* changes — not just the speed. Stripe found non-engineers writing code; the organizational boundary dissolved silently. The follow-up question is: 'when the executor changes from human to agent, what maintains the accountability chain?' In Mira's current architecture, `_DAILY_TASK_CONTRACTS` verify functions only check *whether* a task completed (binary pass/fail). They do not record *which agent type* handled it. If the general agent picks up a writing task because the writer agent is busy or failing, the verify passes — boundary dissolution happens with no trace. Over time, you lose the ability to detect when agents are routinely doing work outside their intended scope.
diff_description: In the task completion state dict (wherever verify result is recorded, likely in task_manager or core.py's daily task loop), add an 'actor' key alongside the existing completion timestamp: e.g., `state['self_evolve'] = {'completed_at': now, 'actor': agent_id}`. The dispatch call already knows which agent it's sending to — capture that string at dispatch time and write it into state on verify-pass. No new logic needed; this is a one-line addition at the dispatch site and one-line read at the verify site. Downstream: the weekly reflect pipeline can then emit a 'boundary dissolution report' — tasks handled by non-canonical agents in the past 7 days.
source_note: 2026-03-28_2-stripe-minions-发现的不是效率是边界消融.md
